question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is considering stopping the implementation of the revised SEND framework.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the projected cost of implementing the TransformEd programme.
To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has calculated the cost of extending the phone-free pilot to every school.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the Priorities for Youth policy review will be completed.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of year 12 pupils deemed ineligible for inclusion in the Summary of Annual Examination Results returns submitted by schools, in the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Back in Business rate relief scheme.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to his Written Ministerial Statement on 27 March 2026, whether he has been accused of bullying by (i) a civil servant within his Department; and (ii) a civil servant who previously worked in his Department.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my recent Written Ministerial Statement.https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/official-reports/written-ministerial-statements/2022---2027/bv191_de_270326.pdf As the Review in question was commissioned and conducted as part of an internal Northern Ireland Civil Service HR process, and as personnel matters are handled by the Department of Finance, it would not be appropriate for me to comment further or to disclose information beyond what has already been placed on the public record.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her work to strengthen the credit union sector.
answer_text: My officials will be briefing the Economy Committee on the outcome of the Credit Union (Modernisation and Reform) public consultation tomorrow, following which the next steps will be finalised and published alongside the consultation response analysis. My Department aims to have a Bill passed within the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her discussions with the British Government in relation to support for households and businesses impacted by the energy crisis.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any work his Department has undertaken to increase awareness of public procurement opportunities.
answer_text: In June 2025, the Executive agreed the Public Procurement Policy Statement, which strengthens our commitment to open, transparent and fair procurement. A key requirement of the Policy is that each Department—either directly or through its Centre of Procurement Expertise—must publish an 18month pipeline of known procurement competitions. This gives businesses greater visibility of what is coming down the line and allows them to plan and invest with more confidence.My Department operates eTendersNI, a single online portal advertising public-sector procurement opportunity valued above £50,000. It is the central point where suppliers can view, search and access tender opportunities from government departments, agencies, arm’slength bodies and the wider public sector.Since July 2025, Centres of Procurement Expertise have taken part in thirteen supplierfacing events to promote awareness of tender opportunities. During this period, we have seen 882 new microbusinesses and 131 new voluntary and community organisations register on eTendersNI—clear evidence of growing engagement.My officials in procurement regularly collaborate with InterTradeIreland and participate in their Go 2 Tender Programmes. My Department’s buyers engage with SMEs and micro businesses through online workshops to highlight opportunities and accessibility to the public sector; providing practical knowledge and advice on how to navigate the public procurement market and prepare better tender bids.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's recent investment in the Lagmore area.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that construction work is ongoing on the Belfast Cycling Network scheme on Lagmore Avenue. This significant investment of £600,000 will deliver improved active travel connections to local schools and amenities in the Lagmore area. The scheme will also have a positive impact in improving road safety through the provision of two toucan crossings at existing crossing points and the introduction of raised table humps at all minor road accesses to further assist pedestrian and wheeled users.This scheme is being delivered alongside a £406,000 carriageway resurfacing scheme due to commence on 18 March 2026 between the junction of Stewartstown Road Roundabout and the roundabout at Mount Eagles Road.Both schemes represent a combined investment of over £1m in the Lagmore area and should be completed in April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools with less than 30 pupils.
answer_text: Data from the 2024/25 Annual School Census exercise indicates that 12 primary schools had an enrolment of fewer than 30 pupils.  Five of these schools have since closed. There were no post primary schools with an enrolment fewer than 30.  Information from the 2025/26 census exercise is scheduled for release on 26 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, before the increase to the cost of school meals effective from January 2026, to detail whether post-primary pupils received a meal, dessert and drink for the published cost of £2.80.
answer_text: Post-primary canteen facilities operate using a cash cafeteria model where each item is individually priced. The allowance provided by the Education Authority (EA) to pupils entitled to free school meals (FSM) rose in January 2026, in line with the rise in the prices charged by the EA, from £2.80 per day to £3.34.  This ensures that all pupils, including those entitled to FSM, can continue to purchase a hot main meal along with a dessert or piece of fruit within this amount. A FSM does not include a drink as free drinking water is available in all canteens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, since the increase to the cost of school meals effective from January 2026, to detail whether post-primary pupils receive a meal, including either a dessert or drink, for the published cost of £3.34.
answer_text: Post-primary canteen facilities operate using a cash cafeteria model where each item is individually priced. The allowance provided by the Education Authority (EA) to pupils entitled to free school meals (FSM) rose in January 2026, in line with the rise in the prices charged by the EA, from £2.80 per day to £3.34.  This ensures that all pupils, including those entitled to FSM, can continue to purchase a hot main meal along with a dessert or piece of fruit within this amount. A FSM does not include a drink as free drinking water is available in all canteens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps his Department has taken to simplify procurement processes, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In March 2025, the Executive approved my proposals to increase the Procurement Control Limits to reflect inflation over the last five years and also to provide greater flexibilities for public bodies. These changes ensure the procurement processes used are proportionate to the level of spend and raise the threshold at which contracts are advertised on eTendersNI. My department also brought forward revised guidance in the format of a Dear Accounting Letter reducing red tape and simplifying tendering processes for grant recipients especially those in the Voluntary Community and Social Enterprise sectors. This was approved by the Executive in October 2025. Building on this, my department revised the policy concerning the procurement of social and other specific services, approved by the Executive in December 2025. This has allowed the procurement process for such services to be simplified and focus solely on below threshold social and community-based specific services with an emphasis on reserving or directly awarding contracts to the Voluntary Community and Social Enterprise (VCSE) sector, or micro businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of how simplified procurement processes would help deliver small local public sector contracts, for example, in schools.
answer_text: In March 2025, the Executive approved my proposals to increase the Procurement Control Limits to reflect inflation over the last five years and also to provide greater flexibilities for public bodies. This raised the threshold at which contracting authorities, such as schools, are required to advertise on eTendersNI to £50,000.These steps have reduced red tape on low value procurement. Contracting authorities, including schools, can now buy what they need quickly and locally for anything under £50,000. They can simply get quotes by email, without having to use the eTendersNI system. For example, if a school needs a joiner to fix a door or a painter to freshen up a classroom, nothing in procurement policy stops them from getting a couple of local quotes instead of inviting formal tenders.This removes a major barrier for public bodies, including schools and will make it easier for them to get the goods and services they need in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new special educational needs regulations.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Social Enterprise Action Plan.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her proposals in relation to protected groups in new hate crime legislation.
answer_text: ANSWERWith your permission, Mr Speaker, I will answer questions 9 and 14 together.  Subject to Executive approval, I intend to introduce the Sentencing Bill to the Assembly very soon. This Bill will include provisions for a new statutory aggravator model for hate crime.The Department has taken an evidence-based approach to inform proposals for protected characteristics in hate crime legislation and I remain committed to delivering the best outcomes for all victims of hate crime in this mandate. Existing protected characteristics (race, religion, disability and sexual orientation) will be retained in the Sentencing Bill.  A power to add further protected groups by secondary legislation (subject to affirmative resolution), should evidence of need arise, will be created. I have also written to Executive Colleagues seeking permission for the inclusion of transgender identity as a protected characteristic for hate crime in the Sentencing Bill.  Based on the statistical data gathered by PSNI and evidence drawn from Judge Marrinan’s public consultation this addition will help increase public safety and afford transgender individuals in our society the same protection in legislation as their counterparts in England, Wales, Scotland and Ireland. Further provisions to support victims of hate crime will be included in the Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill which is scheduled for introduction in 2026.  Policy development work on the additional protected groups of age, sex/gender and variations in sex characteristics is ongoing and will be informed by findings from the Phase 2 consultation which is currently paused until progression of the Sentencing Bill by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the impact of the work undertaken by their Department through the Urban Villages Initiative in West Belfast.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the work taking place under the Inclusion pillar in her seven Foundations for a Better Future.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the efficiency of the youth justice system in the processing of cases involving children and young people.
answer_text: ANSWER AQO 2798/22-27In 2024-25, the average time taken for a charge case to be dealt with at youth magistrates’ courts, was 158 days, a decrease of 6.5% from 2023-24 which was169 days.  In 2024, 1,142 cases were dealt with by the Youth Magistrates Court with only 11 committed to the Crown Court.  Whilst we are working hard to tackle delay in the youth court, it is important to recognise that there has been a significant reduction in the number of youths being received into the youth magistrates’ courts.  In 2024, youth magistrates’ courts received almost 2,000 fewer youths than in 2009 from 3,067 in 2009 to 1,082 defendants in 2024.  An efficient youth justice system is important for victims and witnesses, their families and their communities.  It can also help young offenders to better understand the implications of their actions and create a better opportunity for rehabilitation and reduced rates of recidivism.   The Criminal Justice Board (CJB) have prioritised a programme of work aimed at tackling avoidable delay.  This programme takes a whole system approach, focusing on reducing avoidable delay, reducing demand, freeing capacity and facilitating more proportionate and effective responses to offending behaviours for adults and youths.  It also aims to take advantage of technological developments to improve operational processes and the communication between criminal justice organisations and the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pilots currently active across his Department.
answer_text: There are currently 6 pilots active across the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether he is aware that the school he visited in Israel is located in territory deemed to be illegally occupied, under international law.
answer_text: I am aware that a range of perspectives exist on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education the detail the reason his Department was in communication with the Israeli Embassy in London in relation to what was described as a private trip.
answer_text: The involvement of departmental staff prior to the trip was limited to a small number of administrative tasks, such as confirming attendance, seeking clarification on whether the visit would include any educational components and confirming travel logistics. These actions were considered necessary to manage the trip within my Ministerial diary and to coordinate other official engagements. Private Office staff also obtained IT security clearance to allow me to take my mobile devices out of the country. This action was appropriate and in line with departmental procedure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he advised officials, at any point, of the location of the school he visited during his October 2025 visit to Israel.
answer_text: The name of the school I was visiting was provided to me on Monday 27 October. I provided the name of the school to the department on the same day, when asking for a press release to be prepared.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Northern Ireland Civil Service Office Estate Strategy.
answer_text: The Department is currently progressing an ambitious Office Estate Strategy aimed at reducing the in-scope Civil Service’s office accommodation footprint by approximately 40% by 2028.A progress report on the Office Estate Strategy can be found on my Department’s website. This highlights that good progress is being made.As at 31 March 2025, we have divested 16 properties from the Civil Service estate. This will result in annual rate and rental savings in the region of £3.6 million per annum as well as reducing energy consumption, security and maintenance costs.The Strategy plans for a further four leased properties to be exited by 2028.At present, 4 surplus office properties, all in Belfast — Clarence Court, Victoria Hall, 34 College Street, and Fermanagh House — are on the market for sale.  My Department has agreed Heads of Terms for Clarence Court, Victoria Hall and Fermanagh House, although you will appreciate these are not contractually binding and final sales will be dependent on parties completing.The sale of buildings generates additional capital funding for reallocation to Departments. I would urge public bodies who own properties which are no longer in use and have no identified future use, to work with my department’s Land and Property Service to dispose of these. This is not only important for achieving a capital return, but it also ensures properties are not left derelict and fall into disrepair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) review panels; (ii) working groups; and (iii) advisory bodies, currently funded or commissioned by his Department, including the total cost of each.
answer_text: The table below provides details of the seven Review Panels, Working Groups and Advisory Bodies that are currently being funded by the Department of Education. There are numerous unfunded working groups set up by DE which are currently operational.  They primarily involve officials.  The details of these have been excluded from the answer.The costs of reviews of DE Review Panels, Working Groups and Advisory Bodies have been consolidated, as providing this information for these individually could lead to the release of personal and commercially sensitive information.The total consolidated budgeted costs of the Panels, Groups and Bodies listed comes to approximately £289,000 in 2025-26. The table details how members of these panels, groups and bodies were selected or appointed.All Review Panels, Working Groups or Advisory Bodies commissioned by the Department of Education and requiring funding must have an associated Business Case completed. This Business Case will show the Terms of Reference of the group or panel and detail the objectives along with any funding required and the need for a Post Project Evaluation (PPE) to be conducted.TYPENAMEAPPOINTMENT PROCESSReview PanelIndependent Review of Baseline School FundingMinisterial appointment via Direct Award Contracts based on knowledge, experience and expertise. Advisory BodyInternational Ministerial Advisory PanelMinisterial appointment via Direct Award Contracts based on knowledge, experience and expertise. Review PanelStrategic Review of the Northern Ireland CurriculumMinisterial appointment via Direct Award Contracts based on knowledge, experience and expertise.Advisory BodyCurriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee (TAC)Ministerial appointment via Direct Award Contracts based on knowledge, experience and expertise.Working GroupCurriculum Taskforce Working GroupsThe working groups consists of teachers, curriculum specialists, teacher educators, and CCEA staff. Teachers were appointed through a selection process; others were selected by the Chair of the Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee based on their educational expertise. Review PanelIndependent Review of Assessment PanelMinisterial appointment via Direct Award Contracts based on knowledge, experience and expertise.Review PanelIndependent Review of Teacher WorkloadThe Chair of the Panel was appointed by the Minister based on knowledge, experience and expertise. The other two Panel Members were nominated by the Northern Ireland Teaching Council and Management Side, respectively. All were appointed via Direct Award Contracts
