question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any steps her Department can take to ensure that suitable accommodation is provided, within a reasonable timeframe, when a child or young person is released on bail.
answer_text: AQO 2664 22-27 - ANSWER Where a child perfects bail and is unable to return to the family home or their previous accommodation, the relevant Health and Social Care Trust has a statutory responsibility for securing suitable accommodation under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.  Trusts work hard to achieve this within the shortest possible timeframe, but it is not always straightforward, particularly where a child is not previously known to the Trust.    The Youth Justice Agency has collaborated with the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on a bail fostering scheme pilot.  The aim of the pilot is to provide short-term therapeutic foster placements for children remanded to Woodlands who could not perfect bail because they had no suitable home placement.  My officials are in early discussions with Health colleagues (Department and Trusts) to explore the possibility of extending this pilot to other Trust areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether, in Clause 5 of her Justice Bill, there is an intentional difference between 2(d) he does not cause a serious threat to public order; and 3(d) preventing that person's release from causing a serious threat to public order.
answer_text: The provisions in paragraphs 2(d) and 3(d) of Clause 5 of the Justice Bill were drafted by the Office of the Legislative Counsel (OLC).  The difference between them is entirely due to the construction of the existing legislation and the need for the amendments to integrate appropriately with the current wording.  There is no intentional difference in their meaning:  both are intended to enable police to impose conditions of bail to prevent the threat of serious public disorder.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale of the court having regard to the strength of evidence against the child, as described at 10H, sub-paragraph (2b), in Clause 6 of her Justice Bill.
answer_text: Where a child has been charged with an offence, Article 39 of the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE) provides for the strength of evidence against them to be considered as a factor in PSNI’s decision whether to release the child on bail or to hold them in custody prior to a court appearance.  It is only one of a range of such considerations which should be taken into account.In developing the new youth justice provisions in the Justice Bill, we wanted, as far as possible, to align the factors considered by both police and courts when making a decision to bail or remand a child in custody.  That is why the Justice Bill provisions you reference in Clause 6 introduce changes to the Criminal Justice (Children) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, in order to ensure there is a consistency of approach across the decision-making process.  The considerations set out in new Article 10H will also apply to decisions around the imposition of bail conditions where a child is to be released on bail.  In amending the legislation, we are ensuring that, as far as is practicable, the same considerations can be applied to the decision-making process, whether this is by police or the judiciary.  Ultimately, the best interests of the child will be the overriding factor in any decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department intends to set out the definition of needs, at sub-paragraph (e) (i) in Clause 4 of her Justice Bill.
answer_text: In advance of commencement, the Department is planning to issue comprehensive guidance to all relevant criminal justice agencies to ensure the provisions of the Justice Bill are fully understood and applied appropriately.  It is not our intention, however, to include a definition of needs in this guidance.  The additional considerations being introduced in the Justice Bill, such as a child’s age, maturity and needs, are there to ensure that decisions are made in the best interests of each child in contact with the justice system.  They are also there to ensure a consistent approach in that decision-making process, whether the decision is being made by police or the judiciary.  That is why they not only appear in sub-paragraph (e)(i) of Clause 4 of the Bill, but also in Clauses 5 and 6, all of which deal with decisions on whether to hold a child in custody or release them on bail, and on the application of bail conditions. A child’s needs can differ from person to person and from day to day, and can be a longer-term issue or a short-term immediate need brought about by current circumstances. The professionals who interact with these children are the best placed to assess their needs at that point in time, based on information from a range of different sources.  This could include the child themselves, their parents, carers or other family, appropriate adults, social workers or justice professionals if they are already known to them, and even the victims of their offending behaviour.  Such an assessment should be a dynamic process, and in no way limited to a list or definition issued by the Department; to do so would be to risk being prescriptive and narrow-focused, which is in no-one’s best interests. For these reasons, we do not intend to set out a definition of needs as part of the legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the intent of the word solely, at (2A) and (4) in Clause 8 of her Justice Bill.
answer_text: Clause 8 of the Justice Bill introduces consideration of a child’s accommodation needs into bail decisions taken under the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 and the Criminal Justice (Children) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.  Decisions to hold a child in custody, whether taken by police or courts, should be based on a range of factors such as the child’s age, the nature of their offence(s), their offending history etc.  The use of the word ‘solely’ in Clause 8 is to emphasise that, whilst accommodation can be one such factor that is taken into consideration when making a decision whether or not to hold a child in custody, it cannot and should not be the sole or overriding consideration.  Any decision to remand a child in custody must be because of the seriousness of their offending in the first instance; to do so simply because of the absence of any, or adequate, accommodation for that child is a failure of the system and contrary to the principle of using custody as a last resort.  We recognise that meeting the accommodation needs of children in the justice system can be difficult, and that community provision is not always available.  My Department will be working with the Department of Health and other partners to explore what can be done to increase this provision.  That is why Clause 8 was drafted separately to the other provisions, to ensure it can be commenced by order at the appropriate time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether consideration of a child's community ties and associations, as described at 10H, sub-paragraph (2)(d) in Clause 6 of her Justice Bill, is reasonable and proportionate, given that it could be subjective and relates to circumstances with which a child has limited, if any agency.
answer_text: Where a child has been charged with an offence, Article 39 of the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE) provides for their community ties and associations to be considered as a factor in PSNI’s decision whether to release the child on bail or to hold them in custody prior to a court appearance.  It is only one of a range of such considerations which should be taken into account.In developing the new youth justice provisions in the Justice Bill, we wanted, as far as possible, to align the factors considered by both police and courts when making a decision to bail or remand a child in custody.  That is why the Justice Bill provisions you reference in Clause 6 introduce changes to the Criminal Justice (Children) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, in order to ensure there is a consistency of approach across the decision-making process.  The considerations set out in new Article 10H will also apply to decisions around the imposition of bail conditions where a child is to be released on bail.  Whilst we recognise that a child may, in the majority of cases, have little or no influence over their own community ties and associations, it is nevertheless a legitimate consideration when taken alongside other factors in the child’s life.  Older children, in particular, may be forging certain alliances and associations which are relevant to the offence in question, and which need to be considered as part of the bail decision.  This is especially true when considering the need for bail conditions, for example to prevent such associations taking place to reduce risk of breach or reoffending.  In amending the legislation, we are ensuring that, as far as is practicable, the same considerations can be applied to the decision-making process, whether this is by police or the judiciary.  Ultimately, the best interests of the child will be the overriding factor in any decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his engagement with Treasury ahead of the Autumn Budget 2025.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils who are no longer eligible for free school meals, as a result of the move to Universal Credit.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that it does not hold information as to why pupils would no longer be entitled to free school meals, including whether this is as a result of the move to Universal Credit. It is therefore not possible to provide the information  requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the drug Skyclarys, used to treat Friedreich's ataxia, is available for use in the EU, whether his Department will consider a joint initiative with the Health Service Executive to deliver the drug.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department has (i) costed the expansion of free school meal eligibility for all those with parents in receipt of Universal Credit; and (ii) calculated the number of additional pupils qualifying for free school meals.
answer_text: If all children in households that were in receipt of Universal Credit were to be entitled to free school meals in the 2025/26 academic year, it would be expected to result in over 40,000 extra pupils gaining entitlement to free school meals compared to the 2024/25 academic year. Taking into account the potential loss of income from pupils who would previously have paid for a meal, it is estimated that it could cost up to an additional £22m each year. As there would also be potential for additional pupils taking a free school meal who would not have used the canteen facilities previously, there would also be likely one-off capital costs to facilitate the expansion of capacity in canteen and dining halls that may be necessary depending on the circumstances in each school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline (i) the rationale as to why Clause 12 of the Justice Bill specifies that the period for a youth custody and supervision order must not be less than six months; and (ii) how this specific period of time was chosen.
answer_text: The characteristics of the new Youth Custody and Supervision Order (YCSO) largely replicate those of the current Juvenile Justice Centre Order (JJCO), which is being replaced under plans to update the custodial framework for children. The JJCO has a minimum duration of six months, half served in custody and the other half under community supervision.  During the public consultation on the new order, views were evenly split between those in favour of a set minimum duration and those against.  The majority of those who expressed a preference for a minimum duration felt that six months remained appropriate.  Given that this had worked well in relation to JJCOs for over 25 years, the Department saw no reason to amend the minimum duration for the new YCSO.  A minimum duration of six months, with half of this served in custody, allows time to not only prepare an individual for their release under supervision and put the relevant supports in place, but also to work with them on issues around offending behaviour and to engage them in programmes aimed at assisting with desistance. This approach is borne out by the Youth Justice Agency’s considerable experience and practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the rationale as to why Clause 16 of the Justice Bill provides that a child shall not be ordered to be detained in custody for contempt, unless they are over 16 years of age.
answer_text: The sole purpose of Clause 16 of the Justice Bill is to ensure that any child who is ordered to be detained in custody for contempt of court will be held in a Juvenile Justice Centre.  This amendment is part of a number of changes required to legislation to ensure that my Department delivers on our commitment that no child will ever be held in an adult custodial establishment.  Currently the legislation does not specify where a child detained for this reason should be held; the amendment, therefore, is being introduced for the avoidance of doubt.  It does not introduce any new policy, or amend existing arrangements, in relation to contempt of court, including the applicable age range.  Any intention to do so would require a full consultation process, as would be appropriate for new or amended policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the rationale as to why in Clause 8 of the Justice Bill, at paragraphs (2) to (4), the courts are required to consider a child’s accommodation needs, in respect of the decision to refuse to release a child on bail under Article 10F, when consideration of a child’s accommodation needs have not been explicitly included as a consideration in Article 10F of the bill.
answer_text: New Article 10F, inserted into the Criminal Justice (Children) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 by Clause 6 of the Bill, introduces two very specific conditions or tests for when a court can refuse to release a child on bail and instead remand them in custody.  Unless these tests are met, a court must release the child on bail, either with or without bail conditions.Even if these tests are met, a court can still decide to release a child on bail.  This decision will be based on a range of factors including the child’s age, previous history, personal circumstances etc. These considerations, which are found in new Article 10H, can apply to both the decision to release on bail and the decision to impose (or remove) bail conditions.  The amendments found in Clause 8 of the Bill enable the child’s accommodation situation to be added to the list of considerations in Article 10H.  It has deliberately not been included in Article 10F as a condition or test because the decision to remand a child in custody should be made on the seriousness of their offending, and never on the basis of their accommodation needs.  This means that if the two tests are not met, bail must be granted; if the two tests are met, a court will then consider the other factors – including the availability of suitable accommodation – to help them make a final decision whether or not to remand the child in custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline (i) the rationale as to why Clause 12 of the Justice Bill specifies that the period of detention, specified in a youth custody and supervision order, must not be less than three months or more than one half of the period of the order; and (ii) how these specific time periods were chosen.
answer_text: The Department’s position is that custody for children should be a last resort and for the shortest appropriate period of time.  This may seem at odds with setting a minimum order length of six months, with half of this served in custody.  However, we believe any offence committed by a child which is not sufficiently severe or persistent to warrant at least three months in custody should not result in any custodial disposal but should instead be managed through a community sentence.  Previous research has shown that the application of short orders is not effective and that the ‘short sharp shock’ theory does not have any real benefit in terms of preventing further offending.  These issues were all considered prior to the development of the current Juvenile Justice Centre Order (JJCO), which is being replaced by the new Youth Custody and Supervision Order (YCSO) under plans to update the custodial framework for children.  The Department believes this rationale, which was the basis for setting a six-month minimum custodial order as part of the JJCO, still holds true for the new YCSO, and responses to the public consultation on the issue gave no compelling argument for change.  A duration of six months, with half of this served in custody, allows time to not only prepare an individual for their release under supervision and put the relevant supports in place, but also to work with them on issues around offending behaviour and desistance.  For any sentence beyond the six-month minimum, the new order provides more flexibility than the JJCO in that it does not require the custody/supervision split to be on a 50/50 basis but will allow a greater percentage of the order to be spent on supervision, if the judge deems this to be appropriate.  The period of the order spent in custody will never be more than half of its total duration.  This is in recognition of the importance of the community supervision element of the order, and to align with what currently happens in the adult system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the impact that the 2025-26 budget allocations are having on the delivery of public services.
answer_text: Budget 2025-26 provided earmarked allocations for all of our Programme for Government priorities including reducing health waiting times, childcare, social housing and water infrastructure.Significant progress has been made in reducing long waiting times in health with improvements in areas such as children’s surgery, reflecting a system-wide effort to deliver fairer, faster care. Almost 59,000 additional outpatient, diagnostic and inpatient procedures have been delivered which is well on track to meet the 70,000 target set out in the Programme for Government.Allocations to the Department for Communities have allowed the construction of 1,150 new social housing homes to commence that otherwise would not have been possible.Allocations to the Department for Infrastructure has enabled NI Water to make significant progress, that has exceeded initial plans, on new connections to water and wastewater infrastructure across the North.  Early learning and childcare support measures for families and the sector have been expanded.  This includes the local Childcare Subsidy Scheme. The number of children eligible for support has increased by 60% compared to last year; providing this much needed 15% subsidy on childcare costs to more eligible working parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he will ensure that local councils are not left with unrecoverable costs in relation to tackling dereliction, given that his Department’s own figures put potential enforcement costs at around £4 million a year.
answer_text: The Dilapidation Bill will give district councils new cost recovery powers and the ability to place a charge on a property. The inclusion of robust and effective cost recovery provisions will reduce the likelihood of councils being disadvantaged financially.		On a point of clarification, and assuming the Member has lifted the £4 million cost estimate from the Department’s Regulatory Impact Assessment, this figure relates to total remediation costs for non-domestic properties. This would include remediation, repairs and demolition but excludes council enforcement costs.		Under the current draft of the Bill, a council will have to use its judgment on a case-by-case basis as to whether to begin enforcement proceedings if the likelihood of recovering costs would be minimal, and it is considered reasonable to assume that it would adopt an approach which doesn’t put its finances at risk and ensures it maximises its success in the interest of its ratepayers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he is taking to ensure that the school placements offered to children and young people with special educational needs are (i) appropriate and (ii) timely.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that;To date, the EA has placed 4238 and created 125 Specialist Provisions, and a further 29 classes in our Special Schools for this incoming school year to support children with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN).Over the last 5-10 working days the residual pressure for placements has reduced to around 50 children, and with continued work over the summer, it may be possible to reduce this to zero by the start of the next school year.All parents of children without a confirmed placement will have contact with a Family Link Support Officer until a placement is secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the West Belfast Greenway.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the steps she is taking to address the rise in the number of investigations into police officers alleged to have abused their position for sexual purposes.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any review mechanism his Department operates to ascertain the success of its free school meal policy.
answer_text: As part of my Department’s oversight role, my officials continue to work closely with the Education Authority (EA) in monitoring its operation of the application process for free school meals (FSM). This includes scrutiny of weekly management information provided by the EA on this process. There are also regular meetings with the Head of the EA Catering Service that include discussion of the EA provision of FSM in schools, any relevant operational issues with this provision and how they are being addressed. In addition, the EA also provides the Department with a regular report on its school meals provision, including data on the number of FSM served to pupils.The Department also publishes a regular statistical bulletin on the uptake of school meals in grant-aided schools. This bulletin provides results of the DE data collection relating to the uptake of school meals in grant-aided schools.  The bulletin covers both paid school meals and free school meals and includes analysis of uptake compared to entitlement of free school meals. The next statistical bulletin is due to be published on 5 June. The above actions are all undertaken in order to ensure that the application process for, the provision of, and the monitoring of uptake of FSM is taken forward in a manner that seeks to ensure that those in need can access FSM as easily as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the formula used by his Department to calculate the projected cost of free school meals for a financial year.
answer_text: The Department provides an annual block grant allocation of funding to the Education Authority (EA) to deliver a range of educational services, including the provision of free school meals. It is for the EA to determine how this funding is allocated to ensure sufficient funding is available to provide free school meals, taking into account its anticipated costs in doing so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24347/22-27, to detail the number of soccer pitches with spectator fencing that have been delivered through minor works.
answer_text: As detailed in my answer to AQW 24346/22-27, the Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that in the last five years, it has funded three synthetic pitches at controlled schools with perimeter fencing included. A fourth pitch with perimeter fencing is on site. The Department of Education has progressed pitch upgrades at 14 non-controlled schools – three included ball stop fencing, four included works to perimeter/boundary fence and seven had no fencing works included in the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether a human rights assessment has been carried out relating to the retention of biometric data.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 11 – AQO 1925/22-27			ANSWERYes, my Department, in consultation with the Departmental Solicitor’s Office, has considered the European Convention on Human Rights implications of the provisions to retain DNA and fingerprints that are contained within the Justice Bill.  The provisions are being introduced to address judgments of the European Court of Human Rights and I am satisfied that the provisions address the issues identified by the Court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any departmental initiatives aimed at increasing digital skills.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of sports pitches with spectator fencing have been funded by the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Department of Education’s Building Handbook for Post-Primary Schools requires all synthetic turf pitches to have suitable, robust perimeter fencing.  This is provided to ensure that the site is secure, that use of the pitches is suitably managed and to prevent dog fouling.		As such, perimeter fencing is routinely included on all synthetic pitch projects.  Ball stop fencing is also provided as necessary.	The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that in the last five years, it has funded three synthetic pitches at controlled schools with perimeter fencing included.  A fourth pitch with perimeter fencing is on site.  	The Department of Education has progressed pitch upgrades at 14 non-controlled schools – three included ball stop fencing, four included works to perimeter/boundary fence and seven had no fencing works included in the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Department of Education handbook requires the erection of spectator fencing when a new sports pitch is being delivered for a school or if a sports pitch is being refurbished.
answer_text: Section 10.7.1.8 of the Department’s Post Primary School Building Handbook includes the requirement for fencing of external playing areas. The Handbook provides that:“Suitable, robust fencing shall be provided to ensure that the site is secure, that use of the pitches is suitably managed and to prevent dog fouling. Where necessary and approved by the Department, ball stop fencing should be provided. The extent, height and specification to be agreed with the Department. The synthetic turf playing area shall be surrounded in 3m high, plastic coated, steel mesh fencing, with consideration given to the material behind goal positions for hockey use. Gates and fencing shall be designed so that unauthorised entry, by means of scaling, is deterred. Pedestrian gates shall be 1m wide and the maintenance/emergency vehicle gate shall be 3m wide. The bottom of the fencing around the pitch shall preferably be tied into a concrete plinth or secured with a stop board, if appropriate.”Whilst the Building Handbook is primarily for new builds, it is good practice to meet the requirements when carrying out minor works, when possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is planning to publish a timeline for the early and priority Year 1 actions outlined in his Department's Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: As stated in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan published on 4 February 2025, the early and priority year one actions are either already underway or due to begin imminently.   A number of these actions were subject to the outcome of the NICS Transformation fund, as indicated.  The Finance Minister confirmed on 4 March 2025 that my department’s bid for SEN Transformation funding of £27.5m was successful.  Detailed planning work has commenced and all projects will commence in 2025 and will be evaluated by 2029.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Special Educational Needs Capital Investment Programme.
answer_text: Almost £50m has been invested in my SEN capital programme in 2024/25.  The programme has four strands and an update on each strand is detailed below:MaintenanceA programme of maintenance is being delivered across the special school estate, including an ambitious £2m programme of works to facilitate and improve access to the outdoors including landscaping and new pathways, play and sensory areas.Specialist ProvisionOver 1,450 new places in both special and mainstream schools were created by the Education Authority for September 2024, with building works delivered to establish 114 new specialist provision in mainstream classes in 110 primary and post-primary schools and an additional 67 classrooms created in special schools across Northern Ireland.  School Enhancement Programme In the last six months the business cases have been approved for the school enhancement projects (SEPs) at Thornfield House, Clifton School and Hill Croft School and the business cases for Riverside School is well advanced in the approvals process.  In addition, the construction tender has issued for a £5m extension to Kilronan School.I set aside the funding thresholds for SEPs in special schools and as a result, seven of the ten projects are now to be expanded in scope.  New Special School Provision The technical feasibility report for the approved new special school in Belfast has recently been completed. The planning for new build schools at Sperrinview Special School, Knockevin Special School, a second campus for Ardnashee School and College and a further Belfast Special School has also commenced, with the appointment of technical teams progressing in procurement.  In addition to the above, a long-term capital strategy for the special school estate across Northern Ireland is under development. Individual plans of action for each special school have been developed and are currently being consulted on with stakeholders and the schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his understanding of the practicalities of a special educational needs-first approach to operational planning, as described in his Department's Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: One of the actions in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan under ‘Right Place: Improving the approach to planning for a changing pupil profile’ is: ‘Strategic Area Planning for special education provision across the continuum of provision, based on projected need, ensuring full pathways across all key stages and a SEN-first approach to operational plans’In the context of the area planning, this means prioritising planning for special education provision in line with projected need, right across the continuum of education.  The Education Authority’s (EA) Operational Plan 2 (2024-2026) (OP2) was published on 16 January 2025 and prioritises planning for children with SEN.  In practical terms, this involved detailed planning for increased provision in special schools and specialist provisions in accordance with projections by Local Government District (LGD).  This will be taken forward by the EA working with schools through Area Planning Local Groups (APLGs).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the impact of the proposals outlined in his Department's Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan will be monitored and measured to assess outcomes for children with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: As stated in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan published on 4 February 2025, an outcomes framework that will identify key indicators against which progress will be monitored will be developed in collaboration with delivery partners by Spring 2025.In addition, an independent evaluation will be commissioned and will report at key milestones throughout the implementation of the five-year Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact of the Communities in Transition project in West Belfast.
answer_text: Communities in Transition is part of the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, and since 2019 has delivered over £3.96 million of support to West Belfast.Over the last year alone, approximately 6000 people have been engaged in activities which include one-to-one personal support and relationship building interventions. Data collated shows that through CIT support 80 individuals received one-to-one wraparound support, 18 projects delivered an improvement in relations between the PSNI and communities, and there was a 23% increase in individuals feeling that their communities are now more resilient to criminality and coercive control. This work has been delivered through:Health and Wellbeing					Community Safety and PolicingEmployabilityRestorative Practice, and   				Ex-Prisoner Support.Evidence indicates that these local initiatives continue to be at the forefront of tackling a range of problems caused by paramilitarism and organised crime in West Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the criteria for (a) Minor Works projects; (b) School Enhancement projects; and (c) Major Capital Works; and (ii) to detail the cases where upper spend limits have been exceeded in each of these categories.
answer_text: Minor Works	 The last call for Minor Works was in 2017 and was a joint call between the Department and the Education Authority (EA). Over 6,100 applications were received from schools across all sectors. As there is currently no open call for Minor Work applications, unavoidable projects progressing under the Minor Works programme are currently prioritised on the basis of health and safety, statutory compliance, the need to address urgent and emergency repairs and implementation of approved Development Proposals. 		Schools can submit Unavoidable Minor Works applications for those urgent/essential works which they have identified through their own asset management or risk assessment procedures. However, in many cases, works are initiated by the Education Authority (EA) through health and safety inspections, fire risk assessments, maintenance officer site visits, grounds maintenance officer site visits. 		(ii)	In October 2017 the Department increased the EA’s capital delegation from £500k to £1m. This effectively meant that from that date, all EA capital projects below that threshold were carried out by the EA without reference to the Department. The EA is responsible for determining its own minor works priorities and for planning, managing and carrying out these works. Projects above £1m can still be undertaken but require approval by the Department’s Finance Director.Similarly, all capital projects above £1m in the non-controlled sector, which are managed by the Department’s Investment and Infrastructure Directorate, are subject to approval by the Department’s Finance Director. All capital projects are subject to a value for money business case and relevant statutory approvals. Costs associated with the schemes are reviewed and assessed by Quantity Surveyors to ensure value for money.School Enhancement Programme (SEP)	(i) 	The School Enhancement Programme was established to provide financial support for capital projects with a total capital value of between £0.5million and £4million. There is currently no open call for SEP projects, with the most recent call closing in February 2017.  Details of the selection process and criteria used at that time are available at: School Enhancement Programme - Second Call Protocol for selection | Department of Education	(ii)	The upper spend limit for SEP projects has been exceeded on one occasion to date, at Coleraine Grammar School. This followed approval in 2019 from the then Permanent Secretary to exceed the SEP funding threshold where certain criteria are met in relation to the implementation of approved Development Proposals.In September 2021, as current market conditions were likely to cause many projects to breach the £4m threshold, the Minister agreed that projects would be scoped to a maximum £2.9m construction cost at 2019 rates, with the cash limit increasing in line with prevailing market conditions.Subsequently in October 2022 the Permanent Secretary agreed that SEP funding thresholds could be incr

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how dormant asset funds are supporting the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: Community and voluntary organisations play an important role in delivering vital services and providing support for some of the most vulnerable individuals in our society. Therefore, I am delighted that over £25 million has been awarded from dormant assets funding, under the policy directions sets by my department, to increase capacity, grow resilience and encourage the sustainability of the sector.In Phase One of the fund, £21.5 million was awarded to over 250 organisations across the North, including £1.7m for larger strategic investments to enable collaboration. A further £3.6 million, from the Phase Two Access to Resilience Programme, was announced earlier this month going to 25 network organisations. This will ensure 500 small community grass root organisations can continue to provide practical support to people right across our communities. My department is currently working with the National Lottery Community Fund to develop a third phase of the fund so that it can continue to support the sector in this way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of disability benefit assessments that have been carried out in-house by his Department in each year since 2022; and (ii) how this number equates as a percentage of the overall disability benefit assessments carried out.
answer_text: (i) In-house assessments are currently completed for Personal Independence Payment (PIP) when an award of benefit is being reviewed and where sufficient evidence is available. The total number of PIP, Disability Living Allowance (DLA) and Attendance Allowance (AA) in-house assessments completed in each year since 2022 is shown in the table below. YearsPIPDLAAA2022/238,48117,81213,9062023/2410,75318,81515,5342024/25*5,75210,2218,909TOTAL24,98646,84838,349*PIP statistics up to August 2024, DLA and AA statistics up to October 2024.		(ii) The table below shows the number of in-house PIP assessments as a percentage of all PIP assessments carried out each year.	YearsIn-house assessments as a percentage of all PIP assessments 2022/2313.82%2023/2414.08%2024/25 (to August)19.58%		In relation to DLA and AA claims, 100% of assessments for new claims and award renewals have been carried out in-house during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department is fulfilling its duties under Section 75 of the NI Act 1998, specifically in relation to promoting  equality of opportunity for children and young people.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture Environment & Rural Affair’s Equality Scheme 2021-2025 sets out the actions the Department will take to meet section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, and to ensure that equality is central to our policy and decision-making processes.The Equality Scheme commits the Department to submitting a formal review of progress, on an annual basis, to the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, setting out the actions taken that year. The most recent review reflects progress made between April 2023 and March 2024 and is published online at:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-progress-reports-equality-commission-daera
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of engagement he has had with the Department for Communities in relation to disability benefit assessment services being brought in-house.
answer_text: Engagement initially took place in February 2019 between both Departments on the issue of the Personal Independence Payment (PIP) assessment process, and more specifically its impact on people with learning disabilities as reported to my Department by HSC Trusts.Further limited engagement between officials from both Departments has continued from 2020 to 2022 in regard to a potential partnership approach for the delivery of PIP assessments. However, it was clear that HSC Trusts were unable to take on such a significant function and their priority is to focus on the ongoing challenges they face in delivering health and social care services, which include reducing waiting times for treatment and care across a range of health conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: I am currently engaging with officials on the draft Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan.  I hope to publish the SEN Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider directing the Education and Training Inspectorate to prioritise inspecting schools with Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) has prioritised the inspection of Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS) classes.The ETI recently published Part 1 of its evaluation of the quality of education in SPiMS classes, which is available at:https://www.etini.gov.uk/sites/etini.gov.uk/files/publications/specialist-provisions-in-mainstream-schools-part-1.pdf. This evaluation draws on evidence from visits to 78 primary schools and 35 post-primary schools.  The launch of the evaluation report provided an opportunity to disseminate highly effective practice and the event was attended by over 200 Special Educational Needs Co-ordinators (SENCos).Part 2 of the evaluation of the quality of education in SPiMS classes commenced in September 2024.  It involves inspection of the provision in a representative sample of the 105 schools, with over 120 newly established SPiMS classes, which opened in September 2024.  To date, visits have taken place to over 90 of these classes.  Further visits to the schools will take place throughout this academic year to monitor the quality of education being provided for the children and young people.  This report is due to publish in Term 1 of the academic year 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to Investing in a Better Future, to detail (i) the specific qualification types that are considered low-value by his Department; and (ii) the criteria used to determine their value.
answer_text: All qualifications have a value in the right context.  In a school context, I want the focus to be on qualifications that support every pupil to progress in life, and in particular will support the delivery of the statutory curriculum including the acquisition of knowledge and skills with a specific focus on the essential employability skills of literacy and numeracy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what measures are being taken to encourage young people to join the Civil Service.
To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact the move to Universal Credit will have on the number of children eligible for free school meals.
answer_text: The eligibility criteria for free school meals are set by the Department of Education. 		Therefore, it is not possible for my department to make a specific assessment of the impact the “Move to Universal Credit (UC)” has had, or will have, on the number of children eligible for free school meals, as there are many different reasons why the eligibility criteria may not be met. 		I can confirm that as part of the roll out of “Move to UC”, officials in my department engaged extensively with other Departments to understand the potential impacts on the services they provide, including with the Department of Education in relation to the provision of free school meals.		I understand that the Department of Education will be launching a consultation on the review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the capital programme for special schools.
answer_text: My Department’s Special Educational Needs (SEN) capital investment programme focuses on improving existing provision across the special school estate through a dedicated programme of maintenance, minor works, School Enhancement Projects (SEPs) and new build schools, alongside the creation of additional Specialist Provision in Mainstream (SPiM) classes across mainstream schools in Northern Ireland. For the 2024/25 academic year, 112 new SPiM classes were established in 105 schools with an additional 67 classrooms created in special schools. There are currently ten SEPs progressing at special schools and these have been prioritised and expedited for delivery.  An Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) has been appointed for the first of the up to four new special schools in the greater Belfast area and the technical feasibility is underway for that project.  The planning for new build schools at Sperrinview Special School, Kockevin Special School, a second campus for Ardanshee School and College and a further Belfast Special School has also commenced, with the appointment of ICTs progressing in procurement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the review of his Department's Priorities for Youth will be completed.
answer_text: The review of the current Priorities for Youth (PfY) policy is underway.  The Department has committed to a codesign approach and subject to the ongoing findings of the review, it is currently anticipated that this work will be completed by the end of summer 2026. This includes the time required to complete a public consultation and finalise the new policy.  It should however be noted that where improvements are identified through the policy review process that can be addressed within the current policy, the Department has made it clear that these should not be delayed pending the publication of a new policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 16770/22-27.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 25 October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's engagement with the Department of Education in relation to the establishment of multi-disciplinary teams for special educational needs provision.
answer_text: My officials work collaboratively across numerous structures as part of the End-to-End Review of Special Educational Need (SEN). The Public Health Agency (PHA) continues to work in partnership with the Department of Health (DoH), the Department of Education (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) in developing a collaborative approach to support the identification and management of children and young people (CYP) with identified Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND). This has facilitated cross agency learning, enhanced engagement and interagency working, which is critical in developing a wrap-around approach to supporting CYP at the earliest stage.  This focus has facilitated the development of a number of joint initiatives which have proactively identified and supported children including those with SEND.  Examples include: The Belfast Wide Early Years Programme which is funded by the Department of Education through A Fair Start to pilot a multidisciplinary approach to supporting children with additional needs This targeted early intervention approach supports CYP to maximise their learning potential in advance of starting their primary school education.PHA has led the regionalisation of the Regional Integrated Support for Education in Northern Ireland (RISE NI) teams which work directly in mainstream primary and nursery schools to reduce barriers to learning. RISE NI is an evidence based-tiered model of support which delivers a transdisciplinary model through a multi-disciplinary team operating in each Trust area which includes a range of HSC staff; Physiotherapy; Speech and Language Therapy; Occupational Therapy; Clinical Psychology; and Behavioural Therapists. This model has recently been extended into key stage 2 on a pilot basis in the delivery of the Emotional Health and Well-being in Education framework.  RISE NI has its origins in strong collaborative working and funding across both sectors.  Work is underway to maximise collaborative working and integrated involvement in the development of EA’s Local Integrated Teams to ensure best use of resources across both sectors and holistic and comprehensive management of children’s needs.  The close working relationship between PHA, DoH, DE and EA have been further developed as both sectors respond to the increasing demands within the Special Schools, helping to inform planning and service response.  The PHA have facilitated shared learning/ information events with DE, EA and wider stakeholders on areas of collaborative practice.  The further development of this collaborative approach will be used as a basis of informing and developing future integrated model across the health and education sectors. DE and DOH jointly funded the post of Partnership Lead for Health and Education in SEND to work closely between Health and Special Schools initially. This post, which is delivered by the PHA, has supported the development of an innovative initiative funded by the PHA which harnesses the voi

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact that Artificial Intelligence will have on the economy.
answer_text: Question 10 – AQO 938/22-27Artificial Intelligence can, in the longer term, boost productivity, one of my four economic priorities. Initiatives being taken forward by my department - including the AI Collaboration Centre - will support the adoption of technologies such as AI to enhance productivity and competitiveness in businesses across all parts of the North.I recognise the potential impact on the labour market and the importance of helping people gain the skills and qualifications, that will allow them to benefit from new technologies such as AI. My department and delivery partners provide a range of opportunities for upskilling and reskilling, through for example the £12m Skills Fund.Due to potential economic and societal impacts of AI, I intend to bring a paper to the Executive to stimulate discussion on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the aims of the Draft Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030.
answer_text: The Road Safety Strategy to 2030 aims to provide a framework for government and other road safety partners to establish their own road safety plans, objectives and interventions to eliminate road collisions which result in serious injuries or fatalities. To support the Strategy, an Action Plan has been developed by my Department alongside our Road Safety Partners.  This includes key areas that will have the biggest impact on road safety including, for my Department, a review of drink driving, drug driving and mobile phone legislation. 48 people have lost their lives so far this year on our roads, one life lost is one too many and this Strategy aims to address the challenges that face us all in improving road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the aims of the Draft Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030.
answer_text: The Road Safety Strategy to 2030 aims to provide a framework for government and other road safety partners to establish their own road safety plans, objectives and interventions to eliminate road collisions which result in serious injuries or fatalities. To support the Strategy, an Action Plan has been developed by my Department alongside our Road Safety Partners.  This includes key areas that will have the biggest impact on road safety including, for my Department, a review of drink driving, drug driving and mobile phone legislation. 48 people have lost their lives so far this year on our roads, one life lost is one too many and this Strategy aims to address the challenges that face us all in improving road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of differences in payment rates and managed budgets for clients of Self Directed Support across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My department has always and continues to support the promotion of Self-Directed Support as it allows an individual to have as much choice and control as they wish over their personal budget. A personal budget that has been identified and allocated to them by their HSC Trust to meet their assessed needs.  On 1st April 2024, my department announced the uplift of the regional hourly rate for Direct Payments by 9.8% to £16.88 per hour from the previous rate of £15.37 per hour. This is a set regional rate per hour, and therefore this is the rate that all Trusts give to an individual in receipt of a Direct Payment.  I am aware that there can be a disparity in the rates paid to personal assistants. However, the rate of pay is ultimately a private arrangement between the service user and the personal assistant(s) that they employ, and on that basis the rate of payment to the personal assistant is negotiable. The service user as the employer is responsible for meeting all the administrative costs associated with being a recipient of a Direct payment such as National Insurance contribution, Pension contribution, Sick Pay, Annual Leave, redundancy etc from within the Direct Payment amount allocated to them. Over and above this, my department would endorse that personal assistants receive a rate of pay that is as a minimum reflective of the National Living Wage of £11.44 per hour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to invest in a high-quality teacher professional learning framework for all teachers to help develop specialist knowledge and skills in addressing underachievement and helping to support children with special educational needs.
answer_text: There is nothing more fundamental to pupil outcomes and their educational experiences than effective teaching and learning, and at the heart of this is investing in the educator. Professional development for the whole teaching workforce in Northern Ireland is progressed through my Department’s strategy for Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) “Learning Leaders”. In line with one of the strategy’s policy commitments, we have been working in collaboration with our educational partners to develop a set of proposals, including a TPL Framework and a Learning Leadership Lens, which will incorporate a revised set of teacher competences.  These proposals, including the TPL Framework, are designed to be inclusive and appropriate for all teachers across all schools. They have been developed to empower educators to take responsibility for their own professional learning and development, and to ensure they are equipped to effectively meet the diverse learning needs of all children and young people, including those with special educational needs or at risk of underachievement.My Department intends to initiate a consultation process on the proposals in the near future, in order to seek valuable input from the teaching profession. Furthermore, whilst we are ready to implement these proposals and other TPL initiatives, the Department’s ability to expand and enhance the TPL offering is contingent on additional funding becoming available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reverse the decision to stop the outdoor provision for special schools project.
answer_text: The September 2021 ‘Outdoor Play and Learning for Special Schools’ funding programme was part of the department’s response to COVID-19.  The programme provided £800,000 of investment across the 39 special schools to encourage physical, outdoor play and to enable the purchase of resources to support this. While this programme concluded with the cessation of COVID-19 funding from the UK Government, the department remains committed to addressing requirements across the special school estate to enhance and improve the school environment to ensure increased access to outdoor space to enable physical play for children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps he intends to take to enhance the provision for children with special educational needs to learn through the medium of Irish.
answer_text: Children with special educational needs (SEN) in Irish-medium education (IME) have access to the support services available to all children with SEN across our education system including the Education Authority’s (EA) SEN Pupil Support Services.  There are currently six specialist education classes established across three IME schools.I recognise there may be additional specific provision required to meet the needs of children with SEN in IME and my department is engaging with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) and the IME sector as the End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN progresses to ensure the needs of children with SEN in IME are fully considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what engagement he has had with the Department of Health to further develop, enhance, invest in and commission early years assessment, intervention and support services for younger children with developmental delay and special educational needs, as recommended in A Fair Start.
answer_text: Due to budgetary pressures, Action xi. Key Area 1 of ‘A Fair Start’ was identified as a medium to long-term action.  Work on the early intervention workstream of the End-to-End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) is ongoing.  My officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Health (DoH) in the development of a range of options on improving assessment, intervention and support services for children in the early years with SEN or emerging SEN.   DoH is represented in the governance structures for the E2E Review of SEN.In addition, my department has funded the Belfast Wide Early Years pilot under ‘A Fair Start’, which is a collaborative project between Education, Health and voluntary and community sector partners to support children aged 2-4 years across the Greater Belfast area, who have or may have SEN.  The delivery partners work closely with Allied Health Professionals and the Education Authority’s (EA) Educational Psychology Service to provide assessment, intervention and support services to children and their families through a two-year place-based programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a timeline for the completion of (i) phase two; (ii) phase three; and (iii) phase four of the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: Acknowledging that each of the workstreams under the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) has the ability to move at a different pace dependant on the nature of the workstream, the level of analyses involved, the breadth of stakeholder engagement and the emergence of early findings, the indicative timeline for completion of (i) phase two is 4-6 months; (ii) phase three 3-4 months and (iii) phase four 3-4 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline her plans to introduce no-fault divorces.
answer_text: I am keen to engage with others who may be able to make contributions in relation to no-fault divorces.I have had exploratory discussions with my officials in the Department who are engaged on a range of civil law reform areas including marriage and civil partnership reform and other important policies. I will consider this issue within the available resources and overall legislative priorities, and officials will begin preparatory work with the aim to see progress on this policy area during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps are being taken to ensure that children with special educational needs being placed in mainstream schools are receiving the same levels of support as they would if they were placed in a special school.
answer_text: The Department’s current policy on special educational needs (SEN), which is based on legislation, is that wherever appropriate and considering the wishes of parents, children with SEN should be educated alongside their peers in mainstream schools.  In line with this policy position, 84% of children with SEN are educated in mainstream classes and 16% in specialist education provision in both special schools and specialist provisions in mainstream schools (SPiMS).The support to children with SEN is provided on an individual basis determined by their assessed need.  In addition to school-based support, children with SEN in mainstream schools may access support from the Education Authority (EA) Pupil Support Services (PSS), the specialist setting support team and Allied Health Professionals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reverse the cut of 50 per cent to the Special Educational Needs and Disabilities Transformation Programme made for the 2023-24 funding period.
answer_text: With regard to future funding, you will be aware that departments have not yet been advised of budget allocations for 2024-25.  Whilst the department has been advised of an indicative baseline for planning purposes, detailed budgetary planning has yet to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on work to reduce the timeframe for the special educational needs statutory assessment and statement process from 26 weeks to 22 weeks.
answer_text: The new draft Special Educational Needs (SEN) Regulations must be in place before the statutory timeframe can be reduced from 26 to 22 weeks.  The Regulations are in the final stages of drafting and once all the necessary approvals are in place they will be presented to the Education Committee.  They will then commence their legislative journey through the Northern Ireland Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he plans to publish his Department's response to the findings and recommendations of the Independent Review of Special Educational Needs Services and Processes.
answer_text: IPSOS published their report on the Independent Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) in May 2023.  The recommendations contained within the report are being addressed through the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN which the Department of Education (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) embarked on in early 2023.  The review examines the entire SEN system from a policy perspective to ensure delivery and outcomes are in line with objectives and policy intent. Phase one of the review is complete which involved scoping the review and establishing appropriate governance structures.  Phase two has commenced and primarily focuses on stakeholder engagement to ensure that all decisions and solutions are underpinned by meaningful engagement.  Both phases will be followed by the development of options (phase three) and the way forward which includes an implementation plan (phase four).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether he intends to put in place a process to no longer consider children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) as supernumerary; and (ii) whether he will ensure children with SEN receive school placements well in advance of transition periods.
answer_text: The treatment of children with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) as supernumerary has ensured children with specific needs can access the school most appropriate to meet those needs and do not need to compete for a place with other children.  Nevertheless, I am aware of the increasing numbers of children with a statement of SEN who are educated in mainstream settings and intend to give careful consideration to the current approach and whether it should be changed.  Children on the SEN register but without a statement of SEN (63% of all children with SEN) are admitted to schools in line with the general schools admissions process and, therefore, should receive notification of their school placement at key transition points at the same time as their peers without SEN.  These timescales are published on my department’s website (DE Circular 2023/12).  Children with a statement of SEN (37% of all children with SEN) follow a different process for admission to schools, which seeks to ensure that their school placement is appropriate to meet the needs set out in their statement and subject to any change at the annual review of their statement.  There is a continual flow of children being referred for SEN assessment, which creates challenges in aligning placement of all children with the general schools admissions process.  It is my department’s aim that the majority of children with SEN should receive confirmation of their school placement as the same time as their peers as far as possible.  We are working towards this goal; however, meeting the significant increase in demand for specialist education provision has been challenging in recent years and remains so for 2024/25.  Our special schools are operating at full, and in many cases over, capacity and there is a rising need for more specialist provision in our mainstream schools. The Education Authority (EA) is working at pace to create additional capacity across the education system to meet the demand for September 2024 and to ensure that new specialist education provision is resourced with skilled, capable and competent staff.  The EA are testing advanced planning via annual reviews regarding parental preferences for post-primary in order to obtain indicative data prior to transition years.  Where possible, all children will receive notification of their school placement well in advance of their transition; however, this will be contingent on the availability of appropriate placements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has considered improving the distance threshold for home to school transport for post primary pupils.
answer_text: The statutory walking distances, for the purposes of Home to School Transport, are set out in The Education and Libraries (NI) Order 1986.  These walking distances are broadly similar to other jurisdictions in the UK and ROI.  There are no plans to amend the legislation to change the walking distances at this time.
